title: Reduced motion and input preferences

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Reduced motion and input preferences; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Reduced motion and input preferences
sources.claim: prefers-reduced-motion lets authors reduce or replace nonessential movement while preserving content, state change, and control.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Reduced motion and input preferences
sources.claim: prefers-reduced-motion lets authors reduce or replace nonessential movement while preserving content, state change, and control.

canonicalExplanation.summary: prefers-reduced-motion lets authors reduce or replace nonessential movement while preserving content, state change, and control.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A reduced-motion query removes parallax and uses an instant opacity state change for the same revealed content. Your goal is to verify and repair an experience under reduced-motion, zoom and keyboard-only settings. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Flexbox and Grid; Typography, color and visual treatment; Transforms, transitions and animations; Media and feature queries. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: prefers-reduced-motion lets authors reduce or replace nonessential movement while preserving content, state change, and control. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Reduced does not always mean zero, vestibular triggers vary, and autoplay or canvas motion may need JavaScript coordination. This draft therefore rejects: Reduced-motion users should receive no state feedback because every transition must be removed.

includes: Respect reduced-motion and other user preferences with equivalent non-motion feedback.
Verify and repair an experience under reduced-motion, zoom and keyboard-only settings.
excludes: Reduced does not always mean zero, vestibular triggers vary, and autoplay or canvas motion may need JavaScript coordination.

outcomes: Verify and repair an experience under reduced-motion, zoom and keyboard-only settings.

examples.title: Reduced motion and input preferences: concrete case
examples.situation: A reduced-motion query removes parallax and uses an instant opacity state change for the same revealed content.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A reduced-motion query removes parallax and uses an instant opacity state change for the same revealed content.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Preserve equivalent state feedback while removing or minimizing nonessential spatial and continuous motion.
examples.result: The result follows because Preserve equivalent state feedback while removing or minimizing nonessential spatial and continuous motion.

examples.title: Reduced motion and input preferences: boundary case
examples.situation: Reduced does not always mean zero, vestibular triggers vary, and autoplay or canvas motion may need JavaScript coordination.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Reduced does not always mean zero, vestibular triggers vary, and autoplay or canvas motion may need JavaScript coordination.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Reduced-motion users should receive no state feedback because every transition must be removed.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Preserve equivalent state feedback while removing or minimizing nonessential spatial and continuous motion.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Reduced-motion users should receive no state feedback because every transition must be removed.
misconceptions.correction: Preserve equivalent state feedback while removing or minimizing nonessential spatial and continuous motion.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: Reduced-motion users should receive no state feedback because every transition must be removed.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Reduced motion and input preferences, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Reduced motion and input preferences before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.

recap.summary: You should now be able to verify and repair an experience under reduced-motion, zoom and keyboard-only settings. Keep this boundary in view: Reduced does not always mean zero, vestibular triggers vary, and autoplay or canvas motion may need JavaScript coordination.
